coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have recently been attracting attention in various fields due to their excellent properties, such as biocompatibility, water solubility, low toxicity, fluorescence, and photocatalysis. In particular, CQDs have functional properties such as antibacterial, antioxidant, and UV protection properties and are expected to be used for food packaging and food preservation purposes. Additionally, advanced analytical pathways have become possible with the advent of CQDs, a cost-effective, rapid, and sensitive tool for food safety assessment for the quantification of numerous contaminants, including heavy metals, pesticides, and pathogens. This review provides an overview and perspective on advances in the use of CQDs for food safety and preservation. A detailed summary of the synthesis strategies, raw materials, functional properties, and characterization of CQDs was discussed. This review also covers cytotoxicity studies of CQDs and highlights their importance. The use of CQDs for detecting food contaminants and determining nutritional composition, quality preservation of fresh foods, and functional properties such as antibacterial and antioxidant properties were also highlighted. Additional discussion on the effectiveness of CQDs applied as active fillers for developing packaging materials effective in extending the shelf life of packaged foods was detailed. CQD applications for intelligent and active packaging to ensure food safety were also discussed.

row.entry: Exposure up to 1500 ppm is not toxic

The histopathological analysis revealed that CQDS had no evident harmful effects on mice.
NMR metabolomic profiles, however, showed that CQDS could impact immunological function, cell membrane integrity, and proper liver clearance.
Cell viability was detected at a concentration of 0.128 mg/mL of N-CQD <80%, whereas toxicity increased with increasing concentration in both cell lines.
Adult male rats of the Wistar breed (HEK293 cells)
HEK293 cells exhibited a survival rate of over 90% when concentrated at 500 μg/mL.
Displayed minimal cytotoxicity even at 500 μg/mL when tested against L929 cells.
PEI-CQDs exhibited toxic effects above a concentration of 600 μg/mL.
The Chi/Gel/CD film exhibited little cytotoxicity.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Comparative analysis of CQD-based detection methods with traditional analytical techniques.

row.entry: CQD functionalization with particular compounds or antibodies can improve target analyte selectivity. However, because of possible interaction with other compounds, their selectivity could be hampered in complex food matrices. Selectivity is still being improved by surface modification and sensor design advancements, which makes CQD-based sensors more and more practical for food analysis (Khan et al., 2023a; Tammina et al., 2023).

High selectivity is achieved via chromatographic separation and specialized detection techniques used in HPLC (High-performance liquid chromatography) and GC-MS (Gas chromatography/mass spectrometry). Several column types and mobile phases are used in HPLC to separate components according to their chemical characteristics. GC-MS enables accurate identification of complex combinations in food samples by combining the separation powers of GC with the identification capacity of MS (Hao et al., 2007; Mulubwa et al., 2016).

CQD-based sensors have strong, adjustable fluorescence, which makes them very sensitive. Their capacity to identify pollutants at nanomolar quantities, such as pesticides, heavy metals, and mycotoxins, has been demonstrated in recent investigations. Pesticides in fruits, for instance, have been found using CQD-based sensors at concentrations as low as 0.1 nM (Zarei et al., 2024).

Due to their exceptional sensitivity, HPLC and GC-MS are frequently used to identify analytes in the picomolar to femtomolar range. While GC-MS is excellent at identifying volatile organic compounds, HPLC is especially useful for non-volatile and thermally labile molecules. For instance, pesticide residues in food may be found by GC-MS in quantities as low as parts per trillion (Mulubwa et al., 2016).

Because CQDs are produced using affordable precursors and straightforward synthesis techniques, the process is comparatively inexpensive. In general, the equipment needed for CQD-based detection is less costly than that needed for GC-MS or HPLC. CQD-based approaches are promising for broad application in food safety testing due to their cost-effectiveness, particularly in situations with limited resources (Magesh et al., 2022).

For HPLC and GC-MS systems, the initial setup costs are substantial and include expensive equipment as well as consumables, including columns, solvents, and standards. When compared to CQD-based approaches, these strategies are more expensive due to the additional expenses associated with maintenance and operations. In many food sector applications, their accuracy and dependability outweigh the increased prices (Xu et al., 2021).

simple to use; they frequently call for minimal sample preparation and enable quick on-site testing. For routine food safety inspections and field applications, this is helpful since they don't require a high level of technical competence to operate. Wider use at different stages of the food supply chain is made possible by its user-friendliness (Li et al., 2021).

Significant technical expertise is needed to operate HPLC and GC-MS systems, which include complicated sample preparation and technique development. The requirement for routine maintenance and calibration further increases the operating complexity. Nonetheless, they are indispensable for thorough food analysis in laboratory settings due to their unparalleled resilience and broad analytical capabilities (Bubli et al., 2021).

head.ce:title: Carbon quantum dots: Progress toward food safety and sustainability

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have recently been attracting attention in various fields due to their excellent properties, such as biocompatibility, water solubility, low toxicity, fluorescence, and photocatalysis. In particular, CQDs have functional properties such as antibacterial, antioxidant, and UV protection properties and are expected to be used for food packaging and food preservation purposes. Additionally, advanced analytical pathways have become possible with the advent of CQDs, a cost-effective, rapid, and sensitive tool for food safety assessment for the quantification of numerous contaminants, including heavy metals, pesticides, and pathogens. This review provides an overview and perspective on advances in the use of CQDs for food safety and preservation. A detailed summary of the synthesis strategies, raw materials, functional properties, and characterization of CQDs was discussed. This review also covers cytotoxicity studies of CQDs and highlights their importance. The use of CQDs for detecting food contaminants and determining nutritional composition, quality preservation of fresh foods, and functional properties such as antibacterial and antioxidant properties were also highlighted. Additional discussion on the effectiveness of CQDs applied as active fillers for developing packaging materials effective in extending the shelf life of packaged foods was detailed. CQD applications for intelligent and active packaging to ensure food safety were also discussed.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Today, about one-third of the world's population is suffering from hunger due to food shortage, and on the other hand, a huge amount of food is being wasted. Food waste, which accounts for 47% of all fruits and vegetables and 12% of meat and animal products, is one of the biggest challenges to achieving food security (Halonen et al., 2020). This problem is getting worse due to the increase in world population. An agenda to catalyze action by 2030 aims to minimize food losses in production and supply chains, including post-harvest losses, and halve global food waste per capita at retail and consumer levels (United Nations, 2024).

Food spoilage and food safety are threatened not only by food poisoning but also by microorganisms that can contaminate food, hazards associated with food processing, heavy metals in water sources, unexpected chemicals or substances in food, and numerous toxic pesticide residues in agriculture (Azman et al., 2022). Among the many concerns about food safety, the introduction of hazards by intentional human actions, also known as food fraud or economically motivated adulteration (EMA), is a growing problem. Although developing countries are responsible for the majority of published EMA cases, developed countries have also expressed serious concerns (United Nations, 2024). EMA has been a major problem in pork, poultry, and seafood farming in many countries, including China, India, Bangladesh, and Vietnam (Levi et al., 2020). External estimates from experts put food fraud at $10 to $15 billion annually, or 1% of the global food sector. But experts recently estimated that the annual cost could be as high as $40 billion (Abdel-Motaal, 2024).

Food safety violations leading to dietary dissatisfaction have significant implications for health and economic institutions in developing countries. As a result, the field of food packaging is expanding rapidly, both scientifically and industrially, to comply with increasingly stringent and constantly updated food safety laws. Existing food analysis strategies can meet the routine food analysis needs of laboratories. Still, critical food safety conditions require low-cost, time-saving, rapid analytical methods, including portable, on-site, and convenient test kits. Therefore, the advancement of food safety detection technology is urgently required to ensure food safety.

In this context, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are nanoscale particles with unique size-dependent optical and electrical properties influenced by quantum confinement effects arising from their tiny size (<10 nm) (Khan et al., 2023a).

CQDs offer tunable wavelength, high quantum yield, chemical inertness, photostability, good biocompatibility, and minimal cytotoxicity (Tammina et al., 2023). CQDs possess remarkable quantum yields due to their extraordinary ability to convert light absorption into emission (Yuan et al., 2023). The high sensitivity of these sensors allows the detection of even trace amounts of contaminants or pathogens, including those specifically identified through CQD functionalization (Li et al., 2021; Zhu et al., 2015). The high quantum efficiency of CQDs allows the identification of trace pesticide residues in food, even at concentrations of one part per billion. They exhibit excellent stability compared to existing dyes and fluorescent markers. (Pan et al., 2020). CQDs, which have been shown to be stable against temperature and pH changes and resistant to photobleaching, offer long-term applicability and reliable detection in food safety testing (Khan et al., 2024).

In addition to their excellent antioxidant and antibacterial properties, ease of synthesis, good chemical stability, low toxicity and high compatibility with most biopolymers, they show great promise as an effective filler for making functional food packaging films (Ezati, Khan, Priyadarshi, et al., 2023; Khan et al., 2023b). The hydrophilic bond formed between the CQDs and the polymer matrix significantly improves the composite film's bioactive and physical barrier properties (Ezati, Khan, Rhim, et al., 2023; Khan et al., 2023c). The antioxidant properties of CQDs are attributed to their excellent electron donor and electron acceptor properties (Tammina et al., 2023), and the excellent UV absorption performance of CQDs is due to the abundant π-π conjugated groups in the core (Khan et al., 2023e; Riahi et al., 2024). CQDs also have strong antibacterial activity, as evidenced by ROS generation and photoelectric and photothermal effects. Food waste and other biomass can effectively serve as the main carbon precursors for CQD synthesis. Food waste decomposition poses a significant challenge worldwide. Producing valuable products from food waste has a positive impact on the environment and food recycling (Table 1 ).

This review aimed to provide an overview of the basic concepts of using CQD to identify hazardous food contaminants, monitor changes in food quality, and ensure food safety. First, a brief background overview and properties of CQDs were described, and then the synthesis techniques, material selection, and safety using CQDs were discussed. It was also discussed how food packaging can be improved by incorporating CQDs to indicate food safety, quality, or perishability. Additionally, the potential of CQD-based sensors to identify various target analytes and food additives for safety assessment in a wide range of applications was discussed.

Recently, fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have attracted much attention as new carbon-based nanomaterials with ultrafine diameters of less than 10 nm. CQDs have been widely used in numerous applications in bioimaging, biosensing, chemical sensing, nanomedicine, solar cells, drug delivery, and light-emitting diodes (Zheng et al., 2023). CQDs have emerged as a convenient replacement for inorganic quantum dots due to their rapid and simple preparation and possess several desirable properties, including biocompatibility, low toxicity, hydrophilicity, and optical properties (Park et al., 2020; Sanni et al., 2022). Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are nanoscale particles that possess amazing quantum properties due to their small size. They are typically only a few nanometers in size. Unlike bulk materials, where electrons can move freely over a wide range of energy levels, electrons in CQDs are confined to specific, well-defined quantum states. This confinement is due to the quantum size effect, which occurs when the size of a material is similar to the wavelength of an electron, which causes the electrical properties of CQDs to fall between those of larger bulk materials and smaller individual particles. These intermediate qualities are very interesting as they provide a combination of microscopic and macroscopic behavior. Furthermore, CQDs are capable of absorbing light in the limited UV region between 260 and 320 nm, which is their limited optical absorption range. Because of their selective absorption, they are very helpful in fields where exact control over light interaction is essential, such as bioimaging, sensors, and photonics.

However, Photoluminescence (PL) emission may widen after surface modification, even in the near-infrared region (Zhang & Chen, 2013). Hu et al. presented a procedure for surface modification of CQDs using monoethanolamine as a solvent and passivating agent (Hu et al., 2015). They carefully examined the complex synthetic process at various stages using transmission electron microscopy and obtained some interesting results. When synthesis was started by adding a carbon source to monoethanolamine at room temperature, nanoparticles were not observed at first. Later, when the temperature was increased, larger polymer nanoparticles of approximately 150 nm in size developed at 130 °C (Hu et al., 2015). These nanoparticles experienced dehydration at 150 °C, which dramatically shrank them to 60 nm in size. Then, when the temperature was increased to 170 °C, the 1.5 nm-sized carbon quantum dots were thoroughly implanted into atrophic polymer sheets (Hu et al., 2015). The polymer film was completely removed and the size of the CQDs shrank to 3.5 nm when this temperature was sustained for 10 min. This procedure demonstrates how the size and integration of CQDs within a polymer matrix can be influenced by precise temperature control (Fig. 1 a). This subtle evolution highlights the dynamic influence of temperature fluctuations on shaping the size and shape of the final CQDs.

CQDs possess unique properties such as good conductivity, high chemical stability, environmental friendliness, broadband optical absorption, low toxicity, strong PL emission and optical properties, making them preferable for various applications (Fig. 1b).

CQDs have unique properties related to light absorption, especially in the ultraviolet (UV) band between 230 and 320 nm. Due to the presence of π-conjugated electrons and edge-linked chemical groups in the sp2 atomic structure of CQDs, this absorption extended into the visible spectrum. A peak at 230 nm in the absorption spectra suggests the aromatic C=C bond's π-π* transition and the intrinsic photon-harvesting capability of CQDs. A secondary peak is caused by the n-π* transition of C=O bonds or similar groups, which may be seen at about 300 nm (Arul & Sethuraman, 2018). In addition, an array of parameters, including the hybridized derivatives' structure, content, π-conjugate size, and shape, greatly influence CQD absorption properties. Surface passivation greatly influences the absorption properties of carbon quantum dots (CQDs). Surface passivation reduces defects and improves absorption. Surface functional groups further modify the electronic structure of CQDs. These functional groups significantly broaden and fine-tune the absorption spectrum. Surface passivation and the interaction of functional groups lead to unique and tunable optical properties of CQDs.

CQDs exhibit bandgap fluorescence with an apparent exciton absorption band visible in their UV–vis absorption spectra. A complex interplay of structural, compositional, and morphological parameters determines the absorption behavior of carbon quantum dots. The collective determination of these parameters shapes the electronic and optical properties of CQDs, making them highly versatile materials. Structural features such as size, shape, and crystallinity significantly impact the absorption spectrum of CQDs. Dopants and functional groups within the material composition can also significantly impact the electronic structure and subsequent absorption behavior (Tammina et al., 2023). The shape of a CQD significantly impacts its interaction with light. The tunable absorption response of CQDs resulting from the interaction of these complex elements makes them an attractive option in photonics and nanotechnology (Khan et al., 2023a).

Several influencing properties can modulate the adsorption properties of CQDs. The bathochromic exciton absorption band shift appears consistently, which remains constant as the size of the CQDs increases. This phenomenon reflects the systematic influence of size-related parameters on the exciton absorption properties of CQDs. Moreover, synthesizing CQDs involves introducing several functional groups, such as C=N, O-C=O, C=O, and C-OH, which are critical in determining the adsorption properties. These functional groups produce surface states with energy levels between the π and π* states. Electronic transitions are promoted by dynamic interactions between energy levels and functional groups, which help to modulate the absorption properties of CQDs (Zhu et al., 2012, 2013).

Photoluminescence (PL) is the most crucial characteristic of CQDs, so it is essential to understand the basic principles of these PL properties. While the precise mechanism of PL in CQDs is still unknown, it is believed to result from defect-state and intrinsic-state emissions, which may be affected by quantum confinement effects (QCE) (Zhang et al., 2017), emission traps, oxygen-containing groups, aromatic structures, and edge defects. For instance, Zhi et al. used a self-passivating cross-linker, ethylenediamine, to generate malic acid carbon dots (MACDs) using a microwave (Zhi et al., 2018). By using C-18 reversed-phase silica gel column chromatography to fractionate the MACDs according to their particle sizes, it was possible to examine their optical characteristics, including bandgap energies, by measuring optical bandgap energy (Eg). The study reveals a hydrochromic shift and contraction of the bandgap, as evidenced by a prominent extinction shoulder at 470 nm. When the energy gap between a material's valence and conduction bands is reduced, longer wavelengths of light can be absorbed. The particle size of mesoporous carbon quantum dots significantly impacts the bandgap energy and photoluminescence properties. The interplay between the optical properties and structural aspects of carbon quantum dots is complex. As the particle size decreases, the quantum confinement effect becomes increasingly pronounced, resulting in changes in both the bandgap energy and the emission spectrum. The important role of structural parameters in determining the optical behavior of CQDs is highlighted by these results, emphasizing the importance of precise control over particle size to tailor their properties for specific applications (Zhi et al., 2018).

Due to their excitation-dependent fluorescence, CQDs are an excellent option for analyte detection. Analyte and CQDs interaction can either boost fluorescence by inhibiting the quenching effect or reduce it by quenching. Inner filter effect (IFE), photoinduced electron transfer, energy transfer, and static and dynamic quenching are examples of quenching processes (Zu et al., 2017). In static quenching, the quencher and CQDs combine to produce a ground-state molecule that is not fluorescent. Through charge transfer or energy transfer processes, excited CQDs are brought back to the ground state during dynamic quenching, which occurs when CQDs collide with quenchers. Surface energy transfer, secondary resonance energy transfer, and Dexter energy transfer are three categories of energy transfer. When the absorption and emission spectra of the quencher and the CQDs in the excited state coincide, there is facilitated resonance energy transfer between them; this is recognized as the primary mechanism of CQD quenching. Another factor influencing CQD fluorescence is the IFE. It comes from the attenuation of absorbed radiation or an excitation beam because of the high concentration of CQDs or quenching agents in the solution rather than from a quenching process.

Fluorescence strength decreases with IFE, but decay time is unaffected. The IFE also occurs when the distance between the emitter and re-absorber is more than 10 nm. The absorption peaks of the CQDs would not change since the IFE phenomena do not fall within the dynamic or static quenching process; furthermore, it indicates that no new component is to emerge. As a result, the fluorescence lifespan of CQDs remains unchanged (Liu et al., 2016, 2017).

Tunable fluorescence emissions, or the capacity to emit light over a wide range of wavelengths, are unique characteristics of CQDs. Applications in sensors, imaging, and optoelectronic devices are particularly intriguing because of this tunability. On the other hand, unstable surface imperfections cause CQDs to have poor quantum yields, which results in less efficient light emission. Surface defect instability may result in less radiative recombination, affecting the quantum yield. The technique of surface passivation is used to solve this problem. Using organic or polymeric compounds, such as poly(propionyl ethyleneimine-co-ethyleneimine) (PPEI-EI), to coat the CQDs is known as surface passivation. More steady fluorescence emissions and better quantum yields are the outcomes of this passivation, stabilizing the surface imperfections. PPEI-EI is a surface passivation agent that increases the fluorescence of CQDs in both the solid and solution-like suspension states. The fluorescence emission of passivated CQDs spans a broad spectrum in the visible and near-infrared (NIR) regions. Primarily, PPEI-EI and other surface protectants do not emit light in the visible or near-infrared spectrum. This finding demonstrates that any detectable fluorescence emissions emanate from the passivated CQDs, not the passivation agents. The wide range of wavelengths included in the emission exhibits intensity and properties depending on the excitation wavelength. Due to their tunability, CQDs are helpful for various applications, especially those involving optical and electrical devices (Shen et al., 2011; Sun et al., 2006).

Due to their distinct surface and structural features, CQDs have excellent antioxidant potential that can efficiently interact with and neutralize free radicals present in the reaction medium. Reactive oxygen species (ROS) scavenging, electron transfer, surface functional group interaction, catalytic activity, and hydrogen atom transfer are the main ways in which CQDs have antioxidant activity. First, CQDs can directly scavenge a variety of ROS, including superoxide anions (O2), hydroxyl radicals (-OH), and singlet oxygen (1O2), by donating electrons or hydrogen atoms from their affluent surface functional groups, including hydroxyl, carboxyl, and amine groups. Due to their π-conjugated nature, CDs help transport electrons, efficiently reducing ROS to less reactive species. Delocalized π-electrons inside the sp2-hybridized carbon network of CDs promote this electron transfer mode. Additionally, the functional groups on the surface of CDs engage in redox interactions with ROS, providing hydrogen atoms or electrons to neutralize them. For example, hydrogen atoms on surface amino or hydroxyl groups can neutralize free radicals, converting them into less harmful and more stable molecules. Also, singlet oxygen may be quenched by CDs through energy transfer processes, in which the energy from singlet oxygen is absorbed by the π-conjugated system of CDs, returning it to its ground state (triplet oxygen). Moreover, CDs have an endogenous catalytic activity similar to enzymes such as peroxidase, superoxide dismutase (SOD), and catalase. This catalytic activity enables the effective degradation of ROS by CD, and the overall antioxidant capacity is also improved (Podder et al., 2022).

The strong antioxidant properties of CQDs are well known through their cellular-level free radical scavenging ability. For example, the concentration-dependent free radical scavenging ability of fluorescent N, S-CQDs was investigated using UV–vis light absorption and electrochemical measurements for the Lucigenin-CL test and ABTS (2,2′-azino-bis(3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulfonic acid)and DPPH (2,2-diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl) free radical scavenging activity (Zhang et al., 2018). Zhang et al. applied the Lucigenin-CL test to determine the generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS), including superoxide anion radicals, and the co-doped CQDs exhibited antioxidant efficacy in the xanthine/xanthine oxidase process (Zhang et al., 2018). Ezati et al. used the DPPH and ABTS tests to assess the antioxidant activity of CQDs and heteroatom (B, S, and N) doped CQDs compared with ascorbic acid (Ezati et al., 2022b). At 50 μg/mL, undoped CQDs showed 100% ABTS and DPPH free radical scavenging activity, while ascorbic acid showed antioxidant activity of 80% and 53%, respectively. At a significantly lower concentration of 12 μg/mL, N-doped CQDs showed antioxidant activities of 65% and 30% for ABTS and DPPH, respectively, while ascorbic acid showed a scavenging capacity of approximately 20% in both methods. Due to their excellent dispersibility in aqueous solutions, doping substances, and surface hydroxyl groups, CQDs have excellent antioxidant activity. Through extracellular and intracellular interactions, these free radicals can reduce and quench the oxide, peroxynitrite, and superoxide anion superoxide of CQDs (Dehvari et al., 2019; Moradi et al., 2023; Roy et al., 2022; Tejwan et al., 2021). The strong antioxidant properties of CQDs indicate that they have great potential in various applications where antioxidant activity is required, such as biological systems and food packaging (Ezati et al., 2022a).

CQDs exhibit excellent antibacterial activity due to their unique physicochemical properties, including small size, surface charge, functional groups, and structural defects, and thus can be used in the food industry to inhibit the growth of foodborne bacteria (Khan et al., 2023d). Additionally, CQDs with surface modification and improved functional properties exhibit strong antimicrobial activity against various bacteria, fungi, viruses, and other microorganisms. Another example of adaptability is CQDs' ability to provide antibacterial activity through multiple pathways. Previous studies have described this as a disruption of metabolic activity due to damage to the bacterial cell membrane, resulting in cytoplasmic leakage, ROS generation, and decreased bacterial intracellular ATP concentration (Khan et al., 2023c). The size and type of charge on the surface of CQDs can greatly affect the extent to which CQDs adhere to bacterial cell membranes (Ezati et al., 2022b). For example, Bing et al. prepared negatively, positively, and neutrally charged CQDs using candle soot, spermine, and glucose and compared their antibacterial properties (Bing et al., 2016). They found that positively charged CQDs had the highest rate of bacterial apoptosis, negatively charged CQDs had a less pronounced effect, while neutral CQDs did not affect bacterial growth. Therefore, it can be seen that the surface charge of CQDs has a significant effect on antibacterial activity. Cationic CQDs attract negative charges in bacterial cells, while anionic and neutral CQDs repel them (Bing et al., 2016). Al-Awak et al. suggested the association between the photoactivated antibacterial action of carbon dots with 2,2′-(ethylenedioxy) bis(ethylamine) (EDA-CD) (Al Awak et al., 2017).

The ability of a fluorophore to efficiently convert absorbed photons into emitted photons is measured by its fluorescence quantum yield. Comparing EDA-CD with low observed fluorescence quantum yield (QY) values to EDA-CD with high QY values, the study showed that the latter was less effective in light-activated sterilization. This correlation was observed for model bacterial species that were Gram-positive and Gram-negative. Gram-negative and Gram-positive model bacterial species exhibited increased photoactivated antibacterial activity associated with higher QY values. The bactericidal activity of CQD is greatly influenced by the processes and species involved in generating the emission-excited state of CQD. The efficacy of the photoactivated antibacterial action of CQD was investigated with different experimental parameters, including CQD concentration and treatment duration (Al Awak et al., 2017). These factors have been found to influence the effectiveness of bactericidal CQD. These findings imply that the combination of selected QY, concentration, and treatment duration can optimize the bactericidal efficacy of EDA-CD. Optimizing these settings may improve the ability of EDA-CD to fight bacteria through photoactivation effectively. It has been widely discussed that the superiority of CQDs as effective antibacterial agents is substantially related to the rate of occurrence of hydroxyl functional groups, which are responsible for microbial cell death through the generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS), as shown in Fig. 2 a (Ahmed & Emam, 2020).

The thorough characterization of carbon quantum dots relies heavily on UV–vis spectroscopy, which provides important insights into the surface properties of the particles and makes it easier to understand how they interact with external substrates. UV–vis spectral analysis provides information about possible molecular-level chemical interactions in addition to facilitating the detection of the intact state complex form between CQDs and external substances (Rooj & Mandal, 2023). Cruz et al. synthesized CQDs from citric acid using a pyrolysis technique and thoroughly characterized the synthesized CQDs covering the wavelength range from 265 nm to 450 nm using UV–vis spectroscopy at room temperature (Carneiro et al., 2019). They revealed a unique peak at 340 nm, which was thought to arise due to the n-π* transition induced by C=O bonds. Moreover, this peak revealed information about the influence of different functional groups on the CQD surface (Carneiro et al., 2019). This study demonstrated how versatile UV–vis spectroscopy can be in revealing the complexity of materials composition.

Additionally, Khare et al. (Khare et al., 2018) provided an example of simple monitoring of CQDs doped with various metal ions such as iron, copper, and zinc. Their research focused on creating surface-passivated ZnO-CD, ultrafluorescent CQDs that produce red light and glow brightly due to zinc oxide decoration. These nanoparticles are inexpensive and easy to make, highlighting the value of UV–vis spectroscopy as a reliable method for accurately monitoring the doping process (Khare et al., 2018). UV–vis spectroscopy is essential to fully understand the surface properties, complex formation, and doping processes of CQDs.

A practical and perceptive technique for carefully describing the many surface functionalities present in CQDs is Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR). This spectroscopic technique advances our understanding of the precise applications to which CQDs can be tailored, but it also plays a crucial role during post-synthesis surface modifications, providing real-time insights into the synthesis process (Rooj & Mandal, 2023). Wei et al. (2013) made a significant breakthrough by synthesizing extremely luminous water-based soluble CQDs from paper ash. They used FT-IR analysis to identify several carboxyl, carbonyl, and hydroxyl groups on the resulting CQD surface. This thorough surface characterization provided important information about the chemical composition of CQDs, suggesting possible uses in a variety of fields. Differences in surface structure revealed by analyzing FT-IR spectra are important in determining the QY of CQDs. Capturing this subtle knowledge is essential to successfully synthesize red-emitting CQDs with ideal quantum yields for seamless integration into white light-emitting diodes (LED technology). FT-IR spectroscopy is a sophisticated method that determines the surface features of CQDs and allows customization for applications.

CQDs are small particles (<10 nm), so high-resolution methods are required to investigate their size distribution and structural details thoroughly. One of the important techniques used to explore the properties of artificial CQDs and gain complex insights into their morphology is high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM). Energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS), in addition to HR-TEM, improves our understanding of the morphology of the resulting CQDs (Rooj & Mandal, 2023). Chaudhary et al. synthesized nitrogen and sulfur co-doped CQDs with a 32% quantum yield using a one-step hydrothermal procedure derived from banana juice (Chaudhary et al., 2020). The size distribution of these particles was determined by HR-TEM, which was essential for the detection of copper ions in a concentration range of 1–800 μg/mL with an impressive limit of detection (LOD) of 0.3 μg/mL (Sanni et al., 2022). According to HR-TEM imaging data, the synthesized CQDs showed uniform distribution and spherical morphology, indicating a well-controlled synthesis process. Wang et al 2016. conducted a research study in which they successfully synthesized a new ternary photocatalyst that demonstrated increased photoreaction throughout a wide range of light, including visible, UV, and near-infrared (Wang et al., 2018). They used single atom-dispersed silver and CQDs, co-loaded with ultrathin graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) (SDAg-CQDs/UCN) with single atom-dispersed silver and CQDs, and showed that the synthetic CQDs had an average diameter of around 4 nm and were spherical. As a result, using HR-TEM becomes a crucial method for characterizing CQDs as it offers vital details on their size distribution and shape (Wang et al., 2018).

CQD synthesis methods are generally classified into top-down methods and bottom-up methods (Fig. 2b). This classification is essential to understand the different strategies used for CQD synthesis. Top-down approaches, often utilized in the early stages of research, involve physically or chemically manipulating larger carbon materials to create CQDs. This method involves the intentional removal and slicing of critical carbon structures to generate nanoparticles of the desired size (Wang et al., 2013). Although this approach is somewhat primitive, it is a necessary first step in studying CQDs. On the other hand, bottom-up methods place small organic molecules together into appropriate CQD structures through chemical processes.

The laser ablation method uses high-energy laser pulses to irradiate the target area under thermodynamic conditions where high temperature and high pressure are formed, and the laser is rapidly warmed and evaporated into a plasma state; then, the vapor is crystallized to form nanoparticles (Santiago et al., 2017). A promising way to manufacture CQDs with a restricted size distribution, strong water solubility, and fluorescence properties is through laser ablation. Its expensive and intricate operation, however, restricts its use (Wang et al., 2018). After irradiating the carbon raw material (known as the target material), they treated the product with acid using a Nd: YAG laser source (1064 nm, 10 Hz). After surface functionalization with two distinct oligomers, such as poly(ethylene glycol) and poly(propionyl ethylenimine-co-ethyleneimine), the resulting CQDs were able to demonstrate high-intensity PL emission. Initially, the produced CQDs were not able to emit light. Subsequently, a few research teams effectively showed the synthesis of CQDs with the required size by varying the laser pulse width to regulate the nucleation (Xia et al., 2019). The precise method involves applying a laser beam to a carbon target, ablating the carbon nanoparticles, and producing fluorescent CQDs. Peng et al. CQDs were synthesized using boron nitride as a precursor. For the first time, the photoluminescence of boron nitride quantum dots (BNQDs) produced using a femtosecond laser ablation approach has been tuned from the ultraviolet (UV) to the green region. CQDs synthesized using hexagonal boron nitride (hBN) powder in solvents such as ethanol, diethylamine, and ethylenediamine exhibited three different types of luminescence: ultraviolet, blue, and green. Due to their diverse emission properties, these BNCQDs have high potential as fluorescent probes for bioimaging applications (Peng et al., 2017).

After electrolyte anodization, carbon nanoparticles are extracted from the working electrode at a specific voltage. Carbon nanoparticles become CQDs with fluorescent characteristics after surface passivation. Zhou et al. established a practical approach for producing luminous carbon nanotubes by observing for the first time when a cyclically applied voltage has broken carbon nanotubes to make luminescent carbon nanotubes using an electrochemical method (Zhou et al., 2021). With a spherical shape, tiny size, and narrow dispersion of particle sizes, the produced fluorescent carbon dots exhibited a fluorescence quantum yield of up to 6.4%. With an impressive QY of 11.51%, Joseph & Anappara synthesized white-light CQDs with success (Joseph & Anappara, 2017). An electrochemical reaction inside a battery arrangement between twin graphite rod electrodes made this achievement.

Xu et al. (2004) and Bottini et al. (2005) processed single-walled and multi-walled carbon nanotubes through an arc discharge process to produce three types of CQDs with blue-green light, yellow light, and orange-red light with different quantum yields. The advantage of these CQDs is improved fluorescence performance, but the disadvantage is low yield (Wang et al., 2017). To isolate PL nanoparticles from pristine carbon nanostructures oxidized by nitric acid, Bottini et al. used electronic flash technology. Hydrophobic luminescent carbon nanotube nanoparticles with limited dispersion seemed strange. When carbon oxide tubes are dispersed, fluorescent nanoparticles can collect in water. Carbon nanoparticles were produced in very small amounts by arc discharge. Arc dust often contains complex and difficult-to-extract components (Michelsen et al., 2020).

High-energy ultrasound is used in ultrasonic synthesis technology to produce carbon nanomaterials more quickly in environments such as high temperature and pressure. The creation or modification of CQDs is determined by a combination of parameters of this process, including morphology (surface topography, size, and shape), chemical content, solubility, and aggregation. Changes in CQDs occur due to the creation of acoustic cavities and the sudden rupture of liquid bubbles. Supersonic liquid jets provide high local pressure drops that initiate hydrodynamic cavitation. Macrocarbon compounds are separated into CQDs due to significant bubble formation (Jung et al., 2022). To produce CQDs of the required quality, sonication parameters, such as power, frequency, and sonication duration, must be adjusted. Doping CQDs with metals and other materials can improve their PL and QY qualities. Due to their outstanding physicochemical and fluorescence properties, CQDs can be used in a wide range of applications, including skin lotions, ointments, antibacterial agents, bioimaging, drug delivery, cell labeling, catalysis, polymer synthesis, water treatment, superconducting devices, energy storage devices (Jung et al., 2022). Huang et al. prepared fluorescent CQDs functionalized with terminated polyethylene glycol (PEG) by sonication, and the inclusion of hydrophobic PEG groups improved the dispersibility of CQDs in the aqueous phase and also further improved the biocompatibility of the synthesized CQDs (Huang et al., 2018).

Jiang et al. provided evidence for the single-step synthesis of functionalized CQDs using an arc plasma reactor (Jiang et al., 2010). This method used helium plasma to generate free radicals that guided the synthesis of CQDs and ethylenediamine to functionalize the CQDs in situ. Including primary amine functionality in this approach allows CQDs to be uniformly dispersed in aqueous media. The submerged arc plasma reactor was constructed using a cylindrical glass chamber. Poly(tetrafluoroethylene) caps were used for the cavitation gas supply and electrical connections. To produce CQDs, two cavitating gases, benzene and helium, were introduced into the reactor. Benzene momentarily turned black during discharge and eventually formed CQDs. Precursor concentration and operating voltage mainly determine the chemical composition and size arrangement of CQDs, resulting in changes in PL emission. This method produces CQDs with C=O, C-O, and -OH functional groups, which provide excellent water dispersibility and solubility. These CQDs exhibit intense blue emission in the range of 365–490 nm. By tuning the carbon source, such as reactants doped with nitrogen, boron, and phosphorus, this process can be extended to fabricate photoluminescent CQDs with various compositions. This can improve the QY of CQDs and extend the wavelength of the emission range to red fluorescence (Wang & Hu, 2014).

Microwaves with uniform energy are widely used in CQD synthesis due to their exceptional speed, high efficiency, and ease. The notable ability of carbon-based materials to experience chemical reactions when subjected to microwave treatment enhances their application potential to provide effective and targeted heating. These properties make the carbonization process more advantageous for microwave-assisted carbonization strategies. As a result, this process makes it easier to develop distinct morphologies in nanostructures, which provides a productive way to create CQDs and other nanomaterials (Franco et al., 2020). Using microwave-induced thermal polyamidation and carbonization, Choi et al. pyrolyzed lysine to produce water-soluble CQDs with a QY of 23.3% within 5 min (Choi et al., 2017). Through this study, they demonstrated that branching sites within the precursor are important for fabricating CQDs with significant bulk yield and excellent QY, providing a new avenue to produce CQDs with excellent performance rapidly. Chandra et al. developed a unique technique to generate carbohydrate-generating quantum dots from biodegradable polysaccharides, especially chitosan, alginic acid, and starch (Chandra et al., 2012). These CQDs, named CCQDs, ACQDs, and SCQDs, respectively, were produced using an optimized microwave-assisted heating procedure. This process consisted of separating solutions of chitosan, starch, and alginate and adding polyethylene glycol to each.

The resulting CQDs had an average size range of 2–10 nm and were spherical. In particular, the particle sizes of SCQDs and ACQDs were between 1-2 nm and 2–4 nm, respectively. Highly efficient green synthesis of CQDs from Swartz (Vaccinium meridionale) extract utilizing microwave-assisted carbonization allows the production of large quantities of CQDs at concentrations exceeding 80% mass fraction in approximately 5 min. This process is renowned for producing results quickly and efficiently. According to thermogravimetric analysis (TGA), the resulting CQDs with an average diameter of 30 nm exhibit strong thermal resistance and remain stable in air environments up to 300 °C, suggesting that these CQDs are resilient and can be used in conditions that require extreme heat resistance and effective manufacturing techniques (Franco et al., 2020).

This method of preparing fluorescent CQDs by combining water and carbon sources at high temperatures and pressures has been widely used due to its simple operation. Li et al. used anhydrous citric acid as a carbon source, added ethylenediamine (EDA) and magnesium as passivating agents and chelating agents, heated the mixture in the reactor at 200 °C for 3 h, and obtained water-soluble polychromatic carbon dots of 0.8–2.8 nm with a high fluorescence QY of 83% through dialysis and filtration (Li et al., 2014). The hydrothermal process was easy to use and regulate, allowing for one-step oxidation and modification, which is advantageous for large-scale manufacturing. Ezati et al. produced CQDs using a one-pot hydrothermal process with glucose as the carbon source, and the CQDs were used to prepare chitosan and gelatin (Chi/Gel)-based composite film (Ezati et al., 2022c). The addition of CQDs to the composite film improved its UV light-blocking ability, which is likely due to the innate ability of CQDs to absorb UV radiation for improved UV-blocking function. Their highest optical absorption highlighted the remarkable ability of CQDs to absorb UV radiation at 295 nm. Xie et al. (2019) effectively produced green CQDs by hydrothermal coupling ethylenediamine as nitrogen and barley as a carbon source. The resulting CQDs, with a size range of 4.5–7 nm, exhibited significant wavelength-dependent excitation behavior and blue fluorescence. By using fluorescence quenching to detect Hg2⁺ ions, these CQDs showed excellent sensitivity and selectivity, highlighting their potential for analytical and environmental monitoring applications (Xie et al., 2019).

The standard method to produce CQDs is chemical oxidation, which involves treating carbon compounds with strong oxidizing agents such as hydrogen peroxide, sulfuric acid, and nitric acid. This method is renowned for its economical cost, high yield, purity, and ease of particle size control. Gunja et al. (2019a)produced eco-friendly CQDs using nitric acid (HNO3) as an oxidizing agent and waste car residue (WTR) as a carbon source. The use of strongly oxidizing acids such as HNO3 in CQD preparation poses significant environmental hazards. This method may release toxic gases and create impurities within the sample mixture. The need for additional purification steps to remove excess acids and impurities in CQD synthesis by chemical oxidation increases complexity and cost and limits their widespread use (Gunjal, Gurav, et al., 2019). Although this method is advantageous for industrial applications, it faces challenges due to environmental and economic disadvantages. One of the surprising findings of this study is that it provides a new approach to synthesizing multi-colored, bright CQDs by tuning the fluorescence of CQDs. The ability to tune fluorescence properties adds versatility to CQD synthesis and provides a means to produce CQDs with diverse emission spectra. An innovative process to produce water-soluble photoluminescent CQDs from petroleum coke was presented by Wu et al. (2014). The first step in this process was to oxidize petroleum coke in a solution containing strong nitric and sulfuric acids. The hydrothermal ammonia method was then applied to functionalize the material. In particular, the quantum yield of CQDs significantly increased from 8.7% initially to 15.8% after ammonia hydrothermal treatment. In combination with hydrothermal treatment, successive oxidation and functionalization processes make the carbon quantum dots water-soluble and significantly increase their quantum yield.

Pyrolysis involves the process of breaking down various samples or biological materials by exposing them to an inert environment. This process is almost irreversible. Organic sample materials undergo physical and chemical changes in this inert environment, producing solid residues containing carbon. As a result, pyrolysis often involves exceptionally high temperatures and controlled pressures (Ma, Yin, et al., 2019). Bourlinos et al. produced unique dual fluorescence features of Gd(III)-doped CQDs in the size range of approximately 3.2 nm and pyrolysis process at 250 °C using betaine hydrochloride, gadopentetic acid and Tris base to extract Gd(III)-doped CQDs (Bourlinos et al., 2012). There are several other reports on synthesizing CQDs and GQDs using small organic compounds by pyrolysis or carbonization. Larger CQDs or GQDs are created when small organic molecules are heated above their melting point, causing condensation, nucleation, and further production. These techniques enable heteroatoms to be inherited naturally from their ancestors and are simple, inexpensive, and scalable (Zheng et al., 2023). Murugan et al. used Eleusine coracana to produce CQDs with an average size of approximately 6 nm and high fluorescence (Murugan et al., 2019).

Cu2+ ions were found to quench the fluorescence of these CQDs much more strongly than other metal ions. The preferential adsorption of Cu2+ ions on the CQD surface as a result of interaction with aromatic carbon-carbon bonds was the main cause of the quenching effect. On the other hand, weak bonds are often formed between various parts of the CQD structure and other divalent metal ions. Cu2+ ions and CQDs exhibit strong and selective interactions, indicating the potential of these quantum dots in biosensing for copper ion detection and environmental monitoring. Several variables that affect the pyrolysis process, such as reaction time and temperature, can help us understand how CQDs are formed.

Template methods rely on surface adsorption, reaction, or encapsulation to attach the carbon source to the support material. Through high-temperature pyrolysis, solvent extraction, or acid-base corrosion, the carbon source is converted into carbon quantum dots (CQDs) by removing the support material. Controlling the shape, size, and structure of CQDs using this method improves fluorescence quantum yield and stability. The complex and expensive process involved in using the template method is a major drawback. Removing the template agent and leaving behind the final product is a complex process. This method has a low CQD production yield, which reduces its industrial applicability (Ge et al., 2021). Template insertion can effectively control the size of CQDs and prevent aggregation during the CQD production process, thereby controlling the development of CQDs or preventing the aggregation of carbon nanoparticles. A method of utilizing mesoporous silica spheres as a rigid template was presented by Zong et al. (2011). The unique method involves impregnating silica spheres with a complex solution of complex salts and citric acid. After removal and calcination of the mesoporous support, the remaining carbon dots exhibited excellent monodisperse luminescence and photostability (Zong et al., 2011). Using the soft-hard template method, Yang et al. (2013) achieved uniform morphology and photoluminescence properties for CQDs. The copolymer Pluronic P123 acted as a soft template, whereas mesoporous silica acted as a hard template. They used diamine benzene and 1,3,5-trimethylbenzene as carbon sources for the organic molecules used in the experiment. After template removal, passivation, and carbonization processes, CQDs with tunable size, crystal structure, and improved stability were obtained. CQDs produced through this process exhibited upconversion photoluminescence with efficiencies ranging from 3.3% to 4.7%. Using a soft-hard template effectively suppressed aggregate formation, resulting in CQDs with a narrow size distribution. This method, which allows precise control of size and crystallinity while improving stability and optical properties, is an effective strategy for CQD synthesis (Yang et al., 2013).

A variety of artificial and natural materials composed of carbon have been used as precursors to create carbon dots. Producing carbon dots using conventional bottom-up and top-down processes requires hazardous organic compounds, expensive precursors, toxic solvents, and large amounts of energy, posing complex economic and environmental challenges. Liu et al. (2019) showed that green-processed CQDs have similar properties to chemically synthesized CQDs. The strong electrical and optical properties, strong fluorescence ability, effective quantum confinement, and tunable luminescence capacity of carbon quantum dots are some of these features. Oxygenated functional groups around carbon quantum dots are responsible for the remarkable physiological properties of sustainably produced carbon quantum dots (Liu et al., 2019). Natural carbon sources are cheaper, more readily available, and ecologically beneficial than manufactured ones. CQDs have been prepared using various biomass sources such as bacteria (Hua et al., 2017), paper (Wei et al., 2013), honey (Surendran et al., 2020), leaves (Eskalen et al., 2021), and wheat flour (Fang et al., 2016) (Fig. 3 a).

In recent decades, carbon dot production has increasingly utilized biomass as an environmentally friendly resource (Cui et al., 2019). Recycling of carbon-rich residues is achieved efficiently by synthesizing CQDs using paper industrial waste. CQDs doped with lignin in a ratiometric fluorescent probe to identify biogenic amines (BAs) produced due to food degradation. A practical way to convert biomass waste into carbon-based nanomaterials has been to create CQDs. At a low detection limit of 0.045 μM, this probe responded to BA in a sensitive, fast, and linear manner. The remaining cellulose with crystalline I structure was utilized through oxidation and mechanical processing to produce cellulose nanofibers (CNFs). CNF has also been utilized as a matrix to produce CQD for visual and real-time food freshness monitoring (Dhua et al., 2023).

Microbial biomass, an ecologically harmless resource, has attracted considerable interest as a green biomaterial for synthesizing distinct functionalized CQDs. The high porosity and hydrophilic nature of bacterial nanocellulose make it an ideal matrix for CQDs with antibacterial, photoluminescence, and UV-blocking properties (Kousheh et al., 2020). Although studies on the effective use of specific bacterial strains, including Staphylococcus aureus, Escherichia coli, and Bacillus cereus (Hua et al., 2017; Zhang et al., 2019) for the production of CQDs have been done, the utilization of added value microbial by-products has yet to be explored. The hydrothermal approach was utilized to synthesize and characterize antibacterial CQDs from Lactobacillus acidophilus cell-free supernatant.

In addition, nano paper embedded with CQDs showed more substantial UV-blocking properties and an inhibitory effect on Gram-positive bacteria than on Gram-negative bacteria. CQD is an innovative type of fluorescent antibacterial agent. The UV-blocking ingredients contained in nanocellulose films help create antibacterial and anti-counterfeit packages due to their fluorescent appearance (Kousheh et al., 2020). Li et al. (2019) used the green algae Dunaliella salina to prepare N, S co-doped CDs hydrothermally, and the resulting CDs with an average particle size of 3.2 ± 0.5 nm were subjected to photolysis of methylene blue and methyl violet by visible light, and used for scavenging of 1,1-diphenyl-2-picrylhydrazyl and hydroxyl radicals and selective detection of Fe(III).

In general, carbon-based products are environmentally friendly and have little cytotoxicity when used in everyday life. However, carbon nanomaterials can enter the human body through injection, skin, gastrointestinal digestion, absorption, and respiration, so evaluation of cytotoxicity to the human body is necessary (Khan et al., 2023a). Therefore, it is essential to determine the cytotoxicity of CQDs to ensure their safety in food packaging or biomedical applications. CQDs with excellent biocompatibility combine antioxidant and antibacterial properties and can be produced using environmentally friendly processes from non-toxic starting materials. Their excellent biocompatibility allows them to be used in antibacterial drug administration, food packaging, biolabeling, and cell imaging applications (Namdari et al., 2017; Roy et al., 2021). The cytotoxicity of CQDs has been studied several times at the cellular level (Table 2 ). For example, studies of the possible toxicity of long-term dietary use of CDs made from different compounds in adult zebrafish have yielded encouraging results (Lin et al., 2022). Studies of CDs made from spermidine, ammonium citrate, and lysine have shown no substantial negative effects on adult fish or their offspring. Interestingly, there were no signs of teratogenicity, reduced egg production, changes in mating behavior, weight loss, or delayed embryonic development.

In addition, long-term consumption of lysine carbon nanogel (Lys-CNG), known for its broad antibacterial activity, did not significantly change the composition or amount of intestinal bacteria. Surprisingly, Lys-CNG showed strong bactericidal activity against a variety of bacteria, including S. aureus strains resistant to methicillin and other drugs. These results show that CQDs have a low bioaccumulation potential and are effectively digested and eliminated, reducing the potential health risks associated with long-term exposure to CQDs (Lin et al., 2022).

Studies have shown that low concentrations of CQD solutions do not cause significant harm to human cells. They may even thrive in certain situations. Nevertheless, the cytotoxicity of CQDs tends to increase rapidly over a certain concentration of particles. Concerns have also been raised about CQDs interacting with mammalian cells rather than human cells. Wang et al. (2013) synthesized CQDs using the nitric acid oxidation technique, and the acute and subacute toxicity evaluation of CQDs was performed simultaneously in BALB/c mice and Wistar rats via tail vein injection. This investigation used CQD injectable doses in three groups. In acute toxicity experiments, relevant organs were obtained from BALB/C mice for histopathological analysis. Both male and female rats showed normal behaviour and good physiological condition and showed no obvious signs of poisoning. These results suggest that experimental doses of CQDs did not cause obvious acute toxicity in BALB/c mice (Wang et al., 2013).

Wang et al. prepared CDs using mulberry cocoons (MSC-CDs) by a modified pyrolysis method without external surface protectants. MSC-CDs with abundant surface groups showed excellent solubility and bioactivity. The anti-inflammatory bioactivity of MSC-CD was evaluated through inflammation tests in C57 black mice and female Kunming mice. The results showed that MSC-CD exhibited significant anti-inflammatory bioactivity mediated by inhibiting the expression of interleukin-6 together with tumor necrosis factor-α, indicating that MSC-CD had excellent anti-inflammatory properties (Wang et al., 2020).

Ezati et al. synthesized N-doped and glucose-based CQDs and found that they remained non-toxic and biocompatible in CNF blend films from L929 mouse fibroblasts even after 72 h of exposure (Ezati et al., 2022b). Additionally, digital light microscopy images showed that cells exposed to CQDs and N-CQDs did not show any pathological changes, demonstrating the biocompatibility of CQDs synthesized from non-toxic raw materials when produced in an environmentally friendly manner. According to cytotoxicity studies, CQD doses between 10 and 100 mg/mL resulted in negligible (10–20%) reduction in cell viability and were considered acceptable for food preparation (Ezati et al., 2022b). In cytotoxicity studies of CQDs, the cell system under investigation and the testing technique used for the cytotoxicity assay are important, and additional factors such as surface charge, functional groups, surface coating, shape, and size influence these interactions (Khan et al., 2023a).

Although several studies have revealed the low cytotoxicity and safety of CQDs, the overall safety of CQDs must be ensured through long-term biosafety studies before applying CQDs to areas directly exposed to food or the human body.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Carbon quantum dots for detection of food contamination

ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence capabilities of CQD sensors help detect toxic substances and dangerous microorganisms that contaminate food. The fluorescence on-off-on detection approach makes highly sensitive analysis and detection more accessible (Moradi et al., 2023). Due to their consistent and stable luminescence properties, CQD sensors are considered a suitable probe material for these applications. For example, CQD has been applied to detect Staphylococcal enterotoxin B, aflatoxin B1, and Salmonella typhimurium. Using a fluorescence method, carbon dot-aptamer complexes were formed with 76% quantum yield for specific detection of Salmonella typhimurium (Qu et al., 2018).

Additionally, it was confirmed that S. Typhimurium can be substantially detected in egg samples using CQDs (Wang et al., 2015). It has also been demonstrated that CQDs can be used to detect toxins produced by infection. Hu et al. synthesized CQDs using an eco-friendly one-step synthesis process and used them for selective detection of E. coli O157: H7. When used as a fluorescent probe to identify E. coli in milk samples, the synthesized CQDs exhibited intense green fluorescence near the excitation wavelength of 420 nm (Hu et al., 2021).

Human health can be harmed by pesticide and veterinary drug residues in food, including drug prototypes and metabolites that accumulate in food. One of the key research areas in food safety is the efficient analysis of these residues. CQDs can provide more accurate, effective, and economical fluorescence detection, which has greatly improved the detection of pesticides and veterinary drug residues in food in recent years. For example, a fluorescent sensor based on a combination of CQDs and Fe3+ responded well to ampicillin in milk, pork, and mineral water samples (Fu et al., 2023). Tetracycline (TC) in honey samples could be rapidly and sensitively detected using the MIP-CQD (molecularly imprinted polymers) fluorescence sensor, a single-pore hollow molecularly imprinted polymer prepared using a sol-gel process (Li et al., 2019). Wu et al. created magnetic MIP (MMIP) microspheres functionalized with vinyl phosphate to increase the concentration of pesticide residues, including organophosphorus and combined these microspheres with CQDs to enable fluorescence detection (Wu et al., 2019). Huang et al. developed an original and sensitive ratiometric fluorescence probe using the internal filter effect (IFE) between 2,3-diaminophenazine (DAP) and nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) (Huang et al., 2019). With a focus on the detection limit of methyl parathion, which was set at an astounding 13 ng/g, this unique fluorescent probe showed extraordinary performance in the detection of organophosphorus pesticides (OPPs) in food matrices. The use of the IFE mechanism in this study depends on the specific system components absorbing the emission or excitation of light, which changes the visible fluorescence signal. The detection mechanism for the desired analyte can be illustrated by the fact that the interaction of nanodimensional particles with metals causes extinction or enhancement of the photoluminescence spectral data (Fig. 3b). In contrast, Ahmad et al. used a hydrothermal approach to manufacture CQDs from guar gum with a size distribution of 1.5–6.5 nm and shown sensitivity to Hg2+ and Zn2+ (Fig. 3b) (Ahmed & Emam, 2020). Fluorescence quenching was detected as a function of metal ion concentration. The detection limit was 0–85 mM of M2+, and they demonstrated that fluorescent CQDs are very sensitive to metal at low metal concentrations.

Certain metal ions can interact with CQDs to quench their fluorescence, and many nutrients can chelate metals. The fluorescence of CQDs can be restored by adding these metal ions to the CQD solution. Ultimately, based on this concept, fluorescence-enhanced sensors could be developed to identify nutrients in foods. Zhang, Duan, et al. (2019) used a hydrothermal process to produce branched polyethyleneimine functionalized CQDs (PEI-CQDs) using glucose and polyethyleneimine as raw material components. When GSH (glutathione) is added, Cu2+ can effectively quench the fluorescence of PEI-CQDs, making the system fluorescent again. The PEI-CQDs-Cu2+ system was able to detect both low and high concentrations of GSH, providing significant advantages over existing GSH detection probes. While analyzing real samples, the complexity of food composition affects the accuracy of test results. As a result, it is essential to select appropriate sample preparation techniques and CQDs with improved stability and selectivity.

Ma, Lin, et al. (2019) prepared yellow CQDs using an innovative method using activated carbon as the carbon source. They created an easy and fast method to identify ascorbic acid (AA) using synthesized yellow CQDs. A practical and sensitive AA detection method was developed based on the unique fluorescence properties of yellow CQDs, namely their interaction with Cu2+ ions. The detection limit of 60 μmol/L demonstrated the strong sensitivity of this method. Importantly, this methodology was applied to real-life scenarios, demonstrating its adaptability in examining AA in real ingredients such as vitamin C pills, orange juice, and fresh oranges.

Consuming banned or restricted nutrients and food additives can cause serious health problems in humans. The effective use of CQDs to identify dietary additives and nutrients such as ascorbic acid, tannic acid, carmine, tartrazine, nitrite, glutathione, and sunset yellow has previously been documented to pose significant risks to human health. Residual glyphosate, a hazardous herbicide, poses a serious risk to the environment. To detect glyphosate in the field, Wabg et al. (2016) produced glyphosate antibody-CQDs and showed that the detection limit of glyphosate was 8 ng/mL. The PL spectra of the resulting CQDs showed a linear relationship when the glyphosate content increased between 0.01 and 80 ng/mL. Using CQDs to identify glyphosate in bacteria, acceptable recoveries (87.4–103.7%) were achieved. Li et al. (2016) used N and S co-doped CQDS for effectively translocating pesticides at trace levels. They created a simple, accurate, and effective CQD sensor with a detection limit of 5 ppb to evaluate pesticide residues. The high sensitivity of co-doped CQDs for pesticide detection has been studied and demonstrated in real food tests.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Carbon quantum dots for food quality monitoring

ce:section.ce:para: Biopolymers such as chitosan, gelatin, carboxymethyl cellulose, carrageenan, pectin, cellulose, and starch are most commonly used to prepare CQD-doped films (Ezati et al., 2022a). Their renewable nature, biodegradability, biocompatibility, non-toxic nature and environmental friendliness make it a promising alternative to packaging made from synthetic polymers. We can support the development of new food packaging films that are biodegradable, UVresistant, antibacterial, antioxidant, and biocompatible to preserve food quality and freshness and extend shelf life (Dutta & Sit, 2023). Studies have shown that the inclusion of carbon dots improves the film's UV-blocking ability without affecting water vapor permeability (WVP), mechanical properties, and water contact angle (WCA). ROS, which has a strong antibacterial effect against bacteria and significant antifungal activity, is produced by the composite film. The incorporation of carbon dots into biodegradable polymers improves mechanical parameters such as tensile strength (TS), elastic modulus (EM), and tensile strain of composite films used in food packaging applications (Tang et al., 2019). To date, two main techniques have been used to generate antibacterial CQD-based active films: solvent casting by direct incorporation and absorption/coating (Kousheh et al., 2020; Roy et al., 2021).

Using packaging sheets with antibacterial properties significantly improves food safety and product shelf life. Although many of the other biopolymers used to produce packaging films are inert, chitosan stands out as a popular antibacterial biopolymer. High antioxidant activity, low toxicity and water solubility make CQDs an excellent choice for making active food packaging films with functional fillers (Kousheh et al., 2020; Roy et al., 2021).

Ezati et al. (2022) investigated the potential use of CQDs as functional fillers in the production of active food packaging films. A single-pot hydrothermal process produced CQDs using glucose as the carbon source. Composite films based on chitosan and gelatin (Chi/Gel) were made using CQDs without significantly altering the mechanical properties, WVP, or WCA of the films. The addition of CQDs improved the UV-blocking ability of the film by 99%. CQD-doped composite films also showed high antioxidant activity. The composite film destroyed L. monocytogenes and E. coli, which also produce ROS and thus have antibacterial action against harmful microorganisms (Ezati et al., 2022c). The edible coating of lemon fruit with carboxymethyl cellulose-based CQD (CMC/CQD) film significantly inhibits mold formation and extends the shelf life of the fruit (Riahi et al., 2022). The CQD-added CMC film demonstrated excellent UV protection, high transparency, and enhanced physical and water barrier qualities. Also, the CQD-added films demonstrated significant antioxidant and reliable antibacterial and antifungal action over A. niger and P. chrysogenum, as well as antibacterial activity over L. monocytogenes and E. coli. Even after being stored for 21 days, the lemon coated by CMC/CQD film maintained its outstanding look and did not develop any mold. With its exceptional functional qualities, the CMC/CQD coating film offers many possibilities for active food packaging (Riahi et al., 2022).

Ezati et al. (2022d) used pectin and gelatin to prepare composite films with different ratios of CQDs and found that the composite films containing sulfur-functionalized CQDs had excellent UV-blocking qualities. Transparent packaging films make it easy to observe and evaluate the quality of food contents, but they can also transmit light and degrade the quality of foods that are prone to photodegradation. Therefore, preserving food by blocking certain light wavelengths can extend the shelf life of the product. Reduced vapor barrier quality is one drawback to adding CQDs to packaging films. This can be seen in combinations such as rapeseed protein, gelatin/carrageenan, and gelatin/pectin packaging films (Ezati et al., 2022d; Roy et al., 2021).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Biosensors for real-time detection of food spoilage
ce:section.ce:para: pH-responsive carbon dots have enabled great opportunities for rapid and reliable identification of biogenic amines (BAs) in real-time by the naked eye (Ezati, Khan, Rhim, et al., 2023). When the pH level was increased, fresh tangerine peel/resazurin carbon dots (Tan/Res CDs) showed a pH-responsive color change that shifted from yellow to orange. Tan/Res CDs, with a detection limit of 0.84 μM, demonstrated ammonia detection ability by decreasing fluorescence intensity as the concentration increased from 1 to 100 μM. CDs were coated onto paper strips to provide BA monitoring for pH-responsive intelligent food monitoring. The Tan/Res CDs paper-based sensor showed a noticeable color change from yellow to brown when ammonia vapor was detected. The indicator also changed color to display the ability to identify shrimp spoilage (Fig. 4 ).

In another study, Si et al. used cellulose nanofiber films and lignin-derived carbon dots to monitor real-time and visual food freshness (Si et al., 2022). Lignocellulosic composites were separated using deep eutectic solvents (DES). The extracted lignin was utilized to generate lignin-doped CQDs as a ratiometric fluorescent probe to identify BAs produced when food spoils. Carbon nanofilms can be used to make flexible films for sensing devices. Using this information, they created CQD/CNF composite film, an inexpensive and portable label that can visually confirm the freshness of real samples such as shrimp and pork. In this study, they developed a plan to build a rapid, real-time sensory food quality monitoring and evaluation system and encouraged the development of eco-friendly analysis technology linked to natural products (Si et al., 2022).

One of the important developments in recent years has been the intentional combination of CQDs and aptamers for sensing purposes. Highly specialized oligonucleotide sequences, known as aptamers, exhibit exceptional affinity and specificity for specific target molecules, making them essential for selective screening (Chen et al., 2020). In particular, Du et al. included CQDs and DNA tetrahedral signal amplifiers to detect Staphylococcus aureus (Du et al., 2022). In response to the urgent need for timely detection of S. aureus, a novel and simple biosensing method has been developed. For the sandwich detection of S. aureus, this technique combines blue carbon quantum dots (bCDs) and DNA tetrahedron (Td) fluorescence signal amplifier. Specifically, the tip of the Td structure contains a modified bCD, and the aptamer attached to the Td allows accurate binding to and identification of the S. aureus surface. In particular, this approach resulted in a 4.7-fold increase in fluorescence intensity, indicating improved sensitivity and detection power. With a low LOD of 4 CFU/mL and a linear detection range of 7.22 × 102 - 1.44 × 109 CFU/mL, the biosensor demonstrates improved sensitivity and fluorescence intensity changes. It also exhibits remarkable selectivity, increasing its usefulness in real-world scenarios. Most importantly, the biosensor shows significant stability in real samples with strong recoveries ranging from 96.5% to 104.7%, highlighting its reliability and broad usability (Du et al., 2022). The flexibility of this method is further demonstrated by the fact that it can be easily used in alternative hazard detection setups by simply changing the aptamer.

Nevertheless, it is acknowledged that the signal amplification components used in this experiment have low efficiency. To further improve detection sensitivity and performance, future studies could investigate more effective signal amplification techniques, such as combining more bCD and Td into larger microspheres. This unique combination of aptamers and CQDs highlights a potential direction for biosensing technology, providing improved sensitivity and specificity for a variety of applications (Du et al., 2022). Table 3 presents a comparison between traditional analytical methods and CQD-based detection approaches.

CQDs are novel materials with significant potential to improve food safety. In this review, CQDs were synthesized through various syntheses using biomass waste resources such as food processing by-products or food waste, and the possibility of application to food processing or food packaging was discussed. CQD can be applied not only to food packaging fields such as active packaging and intelligent packaging but also to sensors, imaging agents, and antibacterial agents. CQDs have excellent water solubility, high stability, minimal toxicity, and tunable fluorescence, which are all advantages over common fluorescent nanomaterials. The application of CQDs in food safety has been proven to help prevent microbial growth, detect pathogens, and monitor the freshness of food, among other things. CQD has the potential to become the next guardian of food safety by providing fresh resources at all stages of the food supply chain, from home consumption to agricultural production. Issues such as biosafety and scaling up synthetic technologies must be addressed before widespread commercial launch. Reducing the toxicity of CQDs to much lower levels requires extensive research and creative synthesis methods.

Additionally, the degree of surface passivation, structure, and composition of CQDs vary depending on different raw materials and synthesis methods, which results in different optical, chemical, and physical properties and different susceptibility to biomolecules, heavy metal ions, and acids-bases. The main disadvantage of using CQD to analyze food samples is the inability to recognize foods due to their complex food matrix. In the future, CQD-based food analysis technology should be evaluated to identify nutrients and harmful compounds in food, with a focus on increasing the reaction specificity of biosensing. The ability to produce CQDs from microorganisms or small organic compounds has provided numerous opportunities for the widespread application of CQDs in the food industry. Food waste can be converted into CQD, which can have a positive economic impact on agriculture and also benefit the environment.

However, there are still some challenges in applying CQD. First, the basic photoluminescence process is still difficult to understand and requires further investigation and explanation. Second, the development of CQD synthesis, which allows additional use of waste or other inexpensive biological resources in food processing, has led to a shift in interest toward environmentally friendly and easily accessible raw materials. Future research should focus more on addressing the obstacles associated with converting food waste into environmentally friendly CQDs and using them for food preservation. As research into the utilization of CQDs continues, innovative CQD-based technologies that improve food safety and reduce food loss are expected to emerge soon.
